quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Displacement"?

options.text: The change in position vector Δx = x_final - x_initial; a signed quantity indicating direction, measured in meters
E = KE + PE = ½mv² - GMm/r = -GMm/(2r) for circular orbits; negative means bound
Conversion of mechanical energy to thermal energy by friction: ΔE_thermal = f_k × d
F_ext,net = M_total × a_cm; the center of mass accelerates as if all mass and net external force were concentrated there

quiz_questions.explanation: "Displacement" is defined as: The change in position vector Δx = x_final - x_initial; a signed quantity indicating direction, measured in meters. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Instantaneous velocity"?

options.text: The contact force perpendicular to a surface, adjusting to prevent objects from passing through each other
v(t) = dx/dt; the derivative of position with respect to time, giving the rate and direction of motion at each instant
A frame moving with v_cm; simplifies collision analysis because total momentum is zero in this frame
τ = r × F; magnitude τ = rF sinθ; the rotational effectiveness of a force, measured in N·m

quiz_questions.explanation: "Instantaneous velocity" is defined as: v(t) = dx/dt; the derivative of position with respect to time, giving the rate and direction of motion at each instant. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Instantaneous acceleration"?

options.text: W = ∫F·dr; the scalar integral of force along displacement; measured in joules (J); positive when force and displacement are in the same direction
sinθ ≈ θ (in radians) for small angles; this linearizes the restoring torque and produces SHM
a(t) = dv/dt = d²x/dt²; the time derivative of velocity, indicating how quickly velocity changes
A×B; a vector perpendicular to both A and B with magnitude ABsinθ; used in calculating torque and magnetic force

quiz_questions.explanation: "Instantaneous acceleration" is defined as: a(t) = dv/dt = d²x/dt²; the time derivative of velocity, indicating how quickly velocity changes. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Free fall"?

options.text: P = dW/dt = F·v; the rate at which work is done or energy is transferred, measured in watts (W) = joules/second
J = ∫F dt = Δp; the integral of force over time equals the change in momentum; measured in N·s
Motion under gravity alone with a = -g = -9.8 m/s²; initial velocity can be upward, downward, or zero
A×B; a vector perpendicular to both A and B with magnitude ABsinθ; used in calculating torque and magnetic force

quiz_questions.explanation: "Free fall" is defined as: Motion under gravity alone with a = -g = -9.8 m/s²; initial velocity can be upward, downward, or zero. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Position from velocity"?

options.text: The contact force perpendicular to a surface, adjusting to prevent objects from passing through each other
x(t) = x₀ + ∫v(t)dt; integrating velocity recovers position, with the constant of integration set by initial conditions
Friction converts kinetic energy to thermal energy; work done by friction equals -f_k × d, always negative
E = KE + PE = -GMm/(2r); negative for bound orbits; more negative means a lower, tighter orbit

quiz_questions.explanation: "Position from velocity" is defined as: x(t) = x₀ + ∫v(t)dt; integrating velocity recovers position, with the constant of integration set by initial conditions. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The change in position vector Δx = x_final - x_initial; a signed quantity indicating direction, measured in meters"?

options.text: Static friction
Displacement
Rotational kinematics
Critical speed

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Displacement".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "v(t) = dx/dt; the derivative of position with respect to time, giving the rate and direction of motion at each instant"?

options.text: Instantaneous velocity
Velocity of center of mass
Free-body diagram
Horsepower

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Instantaneous velocity".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "a(t) = dv/dt = d²x/dt²; the time derivative of velocity, indicating how quickly velocity changes"?

options.text: Instantaneous acceleration
Critical speed
Center of mass (discrete)
Net force

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Instantaneous acceleration".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Motion under gravity alone with a = -g = -9.8 m/s²; initial velocity can be upward, downward, or zero"?

options.text: Velocity of center of mass
Energy conservation in orbits
Force-time graph
Free fall

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Free fall".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "x(t) = x₀ + ∫v(t)dt; integrating velocity recovers position, with the constant of integration set by initial conditions"?

options.text: Gravitational field
Position from velocity
Center of mass motion
Impulse-momentum theorem

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Position from velocity".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Kinematics: Motion in One Dimension is accurate?

options.text: Standard formulas exist for common shapes (rod, disk, sphere, hoop) that appear frequently on the AP exam.
Geosynchronous orbits, escape velocity (v_esc = √(2GM/r)), and energy in orbits (E = -GMm/2r) are key applications of gravitational and energy concepts.
The gravitational field g = GM/r² at distance r from a mass M gives the local acceleration due to gravity, explaining why g varies with altitude and location on Earth.
Kinematics: Motion in One Dimension describes the motion of objects along a straight line using position x(t), velocity v(t) = dx/dt, and acceleration a(t) = dv/dt = d²x/dt².

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Kinematics: Motion in One Dimension as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Kinematics: Motion in One Dimension?

options.text: Angular Kinematics describes rotational motion using angular position θ(t), angular velocity ω(t) = dθ/dt, and angular acceleration α(t) = dω/dt.
For constant acceleration, the kinematic equations (v = v₀ + at, x = x₀ + v₀t + ½at², v² = v₀² + 2a(x - x₀)) relate these quantities.
The potential energy function U(x) stores energy that can be recovered as kinetic energy.
The normal force N adjusts to maintain contact and is not always equal to mg—it depends on the geometry and other applied forces.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Kinematics: Motion in One Dimension as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Kinematics: Motion in One Dimension?

options.text: AP Physics C emphasizes calculus-based kinematics, using derivatives and integrals to describe motion with non-constant acceleration.
Power is defined as P = dW/dt = F·v (in watts), connecting force, velocity, and energy transfer rate.

Conservation of momentum applies to collisions, explosions, and recoil in one and two dimensions, and is particularly powerful when forces are impulsive (large and brief) or when the system is isolated.
In vertical circles, the net centripetal force changes with position because gravity's radial component varies.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Kinematics: Motion in One Dimension as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Kinematics: Motion in One Dimension?

options.text: Kinematics: Motion in One Dimension describes the motion of objects along a straight line using position x(t), velocity v(t) = dx/dt, and acceleration a(t) = dv/dt = d²x/dt².
Universal Gravitation states that every mass attracts every other mass with force F = Gm₁m₂/r², where G = 6.674 × 10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg².
Newton's Second Law in its most general form states F_net = dp/dt, which reduces to F = ma when mass is constant.
For an isolated system, the center of mass velocity is constant regardless of internal interactions, making it invaluable for analyzing explosions and multi-body systems.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Kinematics: Motion in One Dimension as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Kinematics: Motion in One Dimension?

options.text: The relationship between linear and angular quantities (v = rω, a_t = rα, a_c = rω²) connects translational and rotational motion.
For constant acceleration, the kinematic equations (v = v₀ + at, x = x₀ + v₀t + ½at², v² = v₀² + 2a(x - x₀)) relate these quantities.
Vectors and Components provide the mathematical language for describing physical quantities with both magnitude and direction.
When non-conservative forces (friction, air resistance) are present, the generalized equation becomes KE₁ + PE₁ + W_nc = KE₂ + PE₂, where W_nc represents energy converted to or from thermal energy.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Kinematics: Motion in One Dimension as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Kinematics: Motion in One Dimension?

options.text: Analyzing car crash data using kinematic equations to calculate braking distances and deceleration rates for traffic safety engineering
Spin cycle in washing machines using centripetal force to push water outward through drum holes for efficient drying
Forensic physics reconstructing car accident speeds from skid mark lengths using W_friction = ΔKE to calculate the initial velocity
Navigation and GPS systems using vector addition to compute resultant displacement from multiple legs of a journey

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Kinematics: Motion in One Dimension.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Kinematics: Motion in One Dimension?

options.text: Computer graphics and game physics engines using dot and cross products for collision detection, lighting calculations, and surface normal computations
Designing roller coasters using calculus-based kinematics to ensure smooth transitions between acceleration phases and safe g-force limits
Forensic physics reconstructing car accident speeds from skid mark lengths using W_friction = ΔKE to calculate the initial velocity
Space elevator feasibility studies computing the potential energy change of lifting payload from Earth's surface to geosynchronous altitude versus traditional rocket propulsion

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Kinematics: Motion in One Dimension.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Kinematics: Motion in One Dimension applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Tracking projectile trajectories in sports science (e.g., baseball launch angle optimization) using one-dimensional kinematic decomposition
Spacecraft trajectory planning using Newton's Second Law with thrust force, gravitational force, and changing mass to compute acceleration profiles
SpaceX and NASA calculating orbital insertion burns using v = √(GM/r) to place satellites at specific altitudes and orbital velocities
Engineering spring-loaded mechanisms (car bumpers, mattresses) by calculating work stored and released using W = ½kx²

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Kinematics: Motion in One Dimension.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Kinematics: Motion in One Dimension?

options.text: Automotive engineering designing braking systems by analyzing deceleration forces and stopping distances using F = ma
Designing pulley systems (block and tackle) to provide mechanical advantage for lifting heavy loads in construction
Analyzing car crash data using kinematic equations to calculate braking distances and deceleration rates for traffic safety engineering
Calculating the work done by a non-constant wind force on a sail using numerical integration of force-displacement data

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Kinematics: Motion in One Dimension.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Kinematics: Motion in One Dimension in practice?

options.text: Wrench design and usage: longer handles provide greater torque for the same applied force, following τ = rF
SpaceX and NASA calculating orbital insertion burns using v = √(GM/r) to place satellites at specific altitudes and orbital velocities
Engineering spring-loaded mechanisms (car bumpers, mattresses) by calculating work stored and released using W = ½kx²
Designing roller coasters using calculus-based kinematics to ensure smooth transitions between acceleration phases and safe g-force limits

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Kinematics: Motion in One Dimension.
